title: Introduction and Research Background
objective: Introduce the research domain of pedestrian crossing behavior at signalized facilities, establish its importance for traffic safety, and outline the scope and objectives of this review

title: Methodological Families in Pedestrian Crossing Research
objective: Systematically categorize and compare the diverse methodological approaches employed in pedestrian crossing behavior research, including simulation, statistical, experimental, and machine learning methods

title: Pedestrian Red Light Running and Violation Modeling
objective: Review and synthesize research on pedestrian signal violation behavior, including behavioral factors, predictive models, and intervention strategies

title: Microscopic Simulation Models for Pedestrian Behavior
objective: Examine agent-based and social force models that simulate pedestrian crossing dynamics, with emphasis on calibration methods and behavioral representation

title: Autonomous Vehicle-Pedestrian Interaction and Communication
objective: Review studies examining how pedestrians interact with and interpret fully autonomous vehicle behavior at crosswalks, including external communication features

title: Behavioral Factors and Psychological Determinants
objective: Synthesize research on individual and social psychological factors affecting pedestrian crossing decisions, including attitudes, norms, and conformity patterns

title: Mobile Phone Distraction and Visual Attention
objective: Examine research on how mobile phone use affects pedestrian visual attention allocation and crossing behavior at signalized intersections

title: Pedestrian Behavior Prediction for Safety Systems
objective: Review probabilistic and machine learning approaches for predicting pedestrian crossing decisions and motion for connected and autonomous vehicle safety applications

title: Research Gaps and Future Directions
objective: Synthesize the identified research gaps from this review, discuss limitations of current approaches, and propose promising directions for future pedestrian safety research
